message:
perf(record): stack-allocate slot scratch for ≤16-column records

`serialize_record_iter_with_precomputed_header_into` previously
allocated a `Vec<&SqliteValue>` plus parallel `Vec<u8>` and
`Vec<usize>` for serial bytes / payload lengths every time it
serialized a row, even for the very common case of small column
counts. For a hot path that's hit on every row write, that's three
small heap allocations per row.

crates/fsqlite-types/src/record.rs:
- Introduce `STACK_PRECOMPUTED_RECORD_SLOTS = 16` and split the
  function into a fast and a slow path:
  - Fast path (slots ≤ 16): three fixed-size arrays
    (`value_refs: [Option<&SqliteValue>; 16]`,
    `serial_bytes: [u8; 16]`, `payload_lens: [usize; 16]`) replace
    the previous `Vec`s. The body iterates the precomputed header
    slots, queries `slot.kind.serial_byte_and_payload_len(value)`,
    accumulates `body_size` with `checked_add`, and stores the per-
    column scratch in those arrays.
  - Slow path (slots > 16): unchanged behavior, extracted into a new
    `serialize_record_iter_with_precomputed_header_append_into`
    helper that keeps the original heap-allocating implementation.
- Both paths still:
  - return `false` when iter is shorter or longer than the slot count,
  - return `false` when payload size overflows,
  - clear-and-resize `buf` to the exact final size before writing,
  - patch runtime serial bytes via `slot.header_offset` for slots
    that need it,
  - encode each value with `encode_serialized_value`.

The fast path covers the bulk of real-world records (most SQLite
rowid/index tables stay well under 16 columns); the slow path
preserves correctness for wider rows.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
